title: String comparison bug
symptom: Expected true for new String("ADMIN"), got false.
explanation: Uses == which compares references instead of content. Strings should be compared with .equals().

title: Trim ignored
explanation: Strings are immutable. trim() returns a new string but result is not assigned.

title: Off-by-one loop error
code: for (int i = 0; i < nums.length - 1; i++) { total += nums[i]; }
explanation: Loop stops early. Should iterate to nums.length.

title: Infinite loop
explanation: count never changes so condition is always true.

title: Concurrent modification
explanation: Cannot modify list during enhanced for loop. Use iterator.

title: Map overwrite bug
explanation: Value resets each time instead of incrementing.

title: Missing null check
explanation: Calling method on null causes crash.

title: Auto-unboxing null
code: Integer x = null; int y = x;
explanation: Auto-unboxing null throws NullPointerException.

title: Lazy stream
explanation: No terminal operation so stream never executes.

title: Stream result ignored
explanation: Stream result discarded. Must collect.

title: Race condition
explanation: Increment not atomic across threads.

title: Finally overrides return
code: try { return true; } finally { return false; }
explanation: Finally block overrides return value.

title: Optional misuse
explanation: Calling get() without check is unsafe.

title: Array out of bounds
explanation: <= goes out of bounds.

title: Wrong map value
explanation: Stores wrong value, should store index.

title: Mid overflow
explanation: Can overflow, use safer formula.

title: Infinite binary search
explanation: Boundaries never move past mid.

title: Wrong pointer movement
explanation: Pointers move in wrong direction.

title: Window never shrinks
explanation: Removes wrong element, doesn't move left pointer.

title: Wrong dp return
explanation: Should return dp[n].

title: Bad base case
explanation: Base case incorrect, should return 1.

title: equals without hashCode
explanation: Must override hashCode with equals.

title: ResultSet misuse
explanation: Must call rs.next() first.

title: Autowired not working
explanation: Spring beans must be injected, not manually instantiated.

title: Missing annotation
explanation: Missing @RestController or @GetMapping annotation.

title: SQL injection risk
code: String q = "SELECT * FROM users WHERE name = '" + name + "'";
explanation: Concatenating input into SQL leads to injection.

title: Sleep misuse
explanation: Sleep is in milliseconds, not seconds.

title: Index misuse
explanation: Last index is size() - 1.

title: Floating point comparison
explanation: Floating point precision makes == unreliable.

title: Missing recursive progress
explanation: Recursive call never changes input.

title: Null stream source
explanation: list may be null before streaming.

title: Mutable key in map
explanation: Changing key after insertion breaks hashing.

title: No timeout set
explanation: Timeouts must be configured to avoid hanging.

title: Two Sum brute force misses final pair
symptom: Valid pair at the end is never found.
explanation: The inner loop stops too early because it uses nums.length - 1. That skips the final possible pair.

title: Merge arrays overwrites unread values
explanation: This only works if nums1 has enough trailing space and the logic respects the original m boundary. Interview merge problems usually require careful in-place handling from the back.

title: Prefix sum starts with wrong seed
symptom: Range sums are off by one element.
explanation: This can be valid for some uses, but many interview prefix sum patterns expect a leading zero slot so range queries are simpler and less error-prone.

title: Anagram check ignores length mismatch
symptom: Different-length strings sometimes pass deeper logic unnecessarily.
explanation: Sorting works, but a fast anagram check should reject different lengths immediately. Missing the early check wastes time and is a common interview oversight.

title: Substring end index misunderstood
symptom: Extracted text is shorter than expected.
explanation: Java substring end index is exclusive. Misunderstanding that boundary is a classic string bug.

title: String concatenation in loop is too expensive
symptom: Solution times out on large input.
explanation: Repeated string concatenation in a loop creates many temporary objects. Interviewers often expect StringBuilder for this pattern.

title: Remove nth node forgets dummy head
explanation: Without a dummy head, edge cases like removing the first node are awkward and often break. This is a common linked list interview bug.

title: Reverse list loses remaining nodes
explanation: The next pointer is overwritten before storing the original next node. Once that happens, the rest of the list can be lost.

title: Merged list never advances tail
symptom: Output list contains repeated or missing nodes.
explanation: In merge-list patterns, forgetting to move tail after attaching a node corrupts the merged chain.

title: Max depth uses min instead of max
symptom: Depth is too small on unbalanced trees.
explanation: Maximum depth must use the deeper subtree, not the shallower one.

title: Level order mixes nodes across levels
symptom: All nodes appear in one level bucket.
explanation: Using queue.isEmpty() for the inner loop consumes the entire queue, so level boundaries are lost. Level order traversal needs the current level size snapshot.

title: Path sum mutates target incorrectly
code:
target -= node.val;
return hasPath(node.left, target) || hasPath(node.right, target);
explanation: Mutating a shared target variable can be fine locally, but interview solutions often break when the value is reused carelessly across branches. Passing a derived value explicitly is safer.

title: Topological sort ignores indegree updates
explanation: Neighbors must have their indegree decremented before checking whether they are ready. Forgetting that step breaks Kahn's algorithm.

title: Union-find find method skips compression assignment
symptom: Solution is much slower than expected.
explanation: The recursive result is not assigned back to parent[x], so path compression never actually happens.

title: Dijkstra uses FIFO queue
symptom: Shortest path answers are wrong on weighted graphs.
explanation: Dijkstra requires a priority queue ordered by current distance. A normal FIFO queue breaks the algorithm on weighted graphs.

title: Min stack loses previous minimum
explanation: Tracking only a single min value is not enough when elements are popped. Interview min-stack problems need historical minimum tracking too.

title: Queue with stacks returns wrong front
symptom: Peek does not match expected queue order.
explanation: In a queue-built-from-stacks problem, peek logic must respect transfer order. Reading directly from the input stack usually returns the newest item, not the oldest.

title: Kth largest heap keeps wrong size
symptom: Returned kth largest value is incorrect.
explanation: Polling when size is equal to k removes one element too early. The heap should usually only shrink when its size exceeds k.

title: Merge k lists heap omits next node
explanation: In merge-k-lists patterns, after polling a node you must push its next node into the heap. Forgetting that loses the remainder of that list.

title: Jump game updates reach too late
code:
if (i > reach) return false;
reach = nums[i] + 1;
explanation: The greedy reach update is wrong because it ignores the current index and previous best reach. Jump game solutions must preserve the maximum reachable position.

title: Gas station running tank resets incorrectly
explanation: When the tank goes negative, the next candidate start should be i + 1, not i. Off-by-one resets are common in this greedy problem.

title: 0/1 knapsack reuses same item multiple times
symptom: Score is too high compared with valid choices.
explanation: For 0/1 knapsack, iterating forward can reuse the same item within the same round. The capacity loop should usually run backward.

title: Palindrome DP reads uncomputed state
explanation: This fill order can read dp[i + 1][j - 1] before it has been computed. Many palindrome DP solutions require iterating i backward.

title: Subsets result stores same list reference
symptom: All stored subsets end up identical.
explanation: Backtracking state is mutable. Adding the same list reference means later changes affect every stored result. A copy is needed.

title: Permutation backtracking never unmarks used value
explanation: The current element is removed, but used[i] is never reset to false. That blocks the value from future branches.

title: Prime checker loops too far and too slowly
symptom: Prime check is slower than expected.
explanation: This works for many values but is inefficient. Interview prime checks usually only iterate up to sqrt(n).

title: Fast power halves exponent incorrectly
symptom: Power result is wrong for odd exponents.
code:
double half = pow(x, n / 2);
return half * half;
explanation: Exponentiation by squaring must handle odd exponents explicitly. Returning only half * half drops one factor of x for odd n.

title: Merge sort merge never copies right remainder
symptom: Sorted output is missing tail elements.
explanation: After the main merge loop, any remaining elements on either side must still be copied. Forgetting the remainder is a common merge-sort bug.

title: Quick sort partition forgets pivot placement
explanation: The pivot must usually be swapped into its final position before returning the partition index. Otherwise the partition is incomplete.

title: Subclass constructor forgets super requirements
symptom: Code does not compile after subclass creation.
code:
class Animal { Animal(String name) {} }
class Dog extends Animal { Dog() {} }
explanation: If the parent has no no-arg constructor, the subclass must explicitly call super with the required arguments.

title: Static method assumed to override
code:
class A { static void hello() {} }
class B extends A { static void hello() {} }
explanation: Static methods are hidden, not overridden. Calls are resolved by reference type, not runtime subtype.

title: Inner join drops unmatched rows unexpectedly
symptom: Rows with no related record disappear.
code: SELECT u.id, o.id FROM users u JOIN orders o ON u.id = o.user_id;
explanation: An inner join only returns matched rows. If unmatched users must remain, the query likely needs a LEFT JOIN instead.

title: Aggregate query selects non-grouped column
code: SELECT department, employee_name, COUNT(*) FROM employees GROUP BY department;
explanation: Selecting employee_name without grouping or aggregating it violates standard SQL grouping rules.

title: Bean not discovered by component scan
explanation: If the class is not annotated as a bean or lives outside component scanning, Spring will not discover it for injection.

title: Controller parameter missing request body annotation
symptom: Posted JSON does not bind to object.
explanation: Without @RequestBody, Spring may not bind JSON request content into the method parameter as intended.

title: Authorization check uses user input directly
symptom: User can request another user's data.
explanation: Authorization should be based on trusted authenticated identity, not just a user-controlled request value.

title: Password stored in plain text
explanation: Passwords should be hashed with a strong one-way password hashing function before storage.

title: Retry loop retries forever
explanation: The loop has no retry limit, no backoff, and no fail-fast path, so repeated errors can trap the system forever.

title: HTTP success assumed without checking status code
symptom: Error responses are treated like success.
explanation: HTTP clients should inspect the response status first. Reading only the input stream path can mis-handle non-2xx responses.

title: Non-idempotent retry creates duplicate charge
symptom: Retrying a timed-out request sometimes charges the customer twice.
explanation: A retried request repeats the side effect because there is no idempotency key or deduplication check. Senior backend systems usually make payment-style operations idempotent.

title: Database update and event publish can diverge
symptom: Order row is committed but downstream service never receives the event.
explanation: The database write and message publish are separate operations, so one can succeed while the other fails. This is the classic need for an outbox or similar consistency pattern.

title: Consumer assumes message is processed only once
explanation: Message delivery is often at-least-once, not exactly-once. Consumers need deduplication or idempotent writes to tolerate redelivery safely.

title: Read-modify-write loses concurrent updates
symptom: Inventory occasionally becomes incorrect under concurrent purchases.
explanation: Two concurrent transactions can read the same starting value and overwrite each other. Senior systems handle this with locking, version checks, or atomic SQL updates.

title: Transaction boundary split across external call
symptom: Database commits even when external API call fails halfway through workflow.
explanation: The database transaction cannot roll back the remote side effect. Once external systems are involved, compensation or saga-style thinking is needed.

title: ORM access pattern causes N+1 query explosion
symptom: Endpoint is extremely slow when returning many parent records.
explanation: Lazy-loading related data inside a loop can trigger one additional query per row. This N+1 pattern is a very common senior backend performance issue.

title: Cache never invalidated after update
symptom: Users keep seeing old profile data after a successful edit.
explanation: The write path updates the database but does nothing about any cached copy. Read performance features often require explicit invalidation or refresh logic.

title: Cache miss stampede on hot key
symptom: Traffic spike causes many identical expensive recomputations.
explanation: When many requests miss the same key simultaneously, they all recompute the value. Senior cache designs often use request coalescing, locking, or stale-while-revalidate strategies.

title: Breaking response shape change without versioning
symptom: Existing clients fail after a deployment even though endpoint path stayed the same.
explanation: Changing a response contract in place can break consumers. Senior API design usually treats response shape as a compatibility commitment and versions breaking changes.

title: POST endpoint repeats side effects on client retry
symptom: Network retry creates duplicate resource or duplicate payment.
explanation: Retried create operations can safely happen only when the endpoint or service layer supports deduplication or idempotency semantics.

title: Logs omit request correlation id
symptom: Production incident is hard to trace across services.
explanation: Without a correlation or trace id, logs from a single request cannot easily be stitched together across service boundaries.

title: Error metric never emitted on failure path
symptom: Dashboards look healthy even while requests are failing.
explanation: Logging alone may not update dashboards or alerts. Senior systems often emit explicit metrics for failure rate, latency, and saturation signals.

title: CPU pool blocked by slow I/O
symptom: Service throughput collapses during slow downstream calls.
explanation: Using a small fixed pool for blocking I/O can starve the system. Senior designs separate blocking workloads or use different concurrency models for them.

title: Executor queue grows without backpressure
symptom: Memory usage grows steadily during overload.
explanation: Many convenience executors use unbounded queues. Under load, tasks pile up in memory instead of being rejected or slowed, which can cause OOM conditions.

title: Listener registration never cleaned up
symptom: Heap grows after repeated connect-disconnect cycles.
explanation: Objects held by global listeners remain strongly reachable unless unregistered. Long-lived listener registries are a classic source of memory leaks.

title: In-memory cache grows forever
symptom: Service memory climbs over days of uptime.
code: private final Map<String, Result> cache = new ConcurrentHashMap<>();
explanation: A cache without size limits or eviction policy is really just an ever-growing map. Senior systems bound caches intentionally.

title: JSON deserialization fails without default constructor or creator
symptom: HTTP request body cannot be deserialized into domain object.
explanation: Many serializers need a default constructor, explicit creator annotations, or matching configuration for immutable objects.

title: Bidirectional relationship causes infinite JSON recursion
symptom: Serializing entity graph blows up or loops forever.
code:
class Order { Customer customer; }
class Customer { List<Order> orders; }
explanation: Bidirectional references can cause serializers to walk the same graph indefinitely unless one side is ignored, flattened, or managed explicitly.

title: Consumer assumes global message ordering
symptom: Update event is processed before create event for the same entity.
explanation: Many messaging systems provide ordering only within a partition or key scope, not globally. Consumers must be designed with real ordering guarantees in mind.

title: Poison message blocks queue forever
symptom: Same failing message is retried endlessly and prevents progress.
explanation: Without dead-letter handling, retry policy, or quarantine logic, a single bad message can stall a pipeline indefinitely.

title: Secret hardcoded in source
symptom: Credential appears in repository or build artifact.
explanation: Secrets should come from secure runtime configuration or a secret manager, not be embedded in source code.

title: Sensitive data written to logs
symptom: PII or credentials are visible in application logs.
code: log.info("login request user={} password={}", username, password);
explanation: Logs are often widely accessible and retained for long periods. Sensitive values should be redacted or omitted entirely.

title: Repeated downstream failures cascade into full outage
symptom: One unhealthy dependency drags the whole service down.
explanation: Without timeouts, fallback, or circuit breaking, repeated calls to a failing dependency can exhaust threads and amplify outages.

title: One noisy integration starves unrelated traffic
symptom: Slow partner API causes unrelated endpoints to become slow too.
explanation: Using the same resource pool for unrelated workloads means one noisy dependency can consume shared capacity. Bulkheads isolate blast radius.
